subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: Name the four layers of the Earth, starting from the outside and working inwards.
answer: crust
mantle
outer core
inner core
accept: crust, mantle, outer core, inner core
misconceptions: crust, core, mantle
atmosphere, crust, mantle, core
crust, mantle, core (one core only)

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: What is the crust?
answer: the thin, solid outer layer of the Earth that we live on
accept: the outer layer we live on
the solid outer rocky layer
misconceptions: the hot centre of the Earth
the middle semi-molten layer
the gases around the Earth

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: Describe the mantle and what it is made from.
answer: the thick middle layer of the Earth
made of slow-moving, semi-molten rock
accept: semi-molten / partly melted rock
the thickest layer, made of molten rock that moves slowly
misconceptions: the mantle is made of iron
the mantle is solid rock that never moves
the mantle is liquid water

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: The outer core is liquid but the inner core is solid, even though the inner core is hotter. Explain why the inner core is solid.
answer: the pressure at the centre is so great
that the iron particles are squeezed together as a solid
accept: the huge pressure squeezes the particles into a solid
pressure is so high it forces the iron to stay solid
misconceptions: the inner core is colder than the outer core
the inner core has cooled down and frozen
the inner core is made of a different metal that does not melt

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: Which metal makes up most of the Earth's core?
answer: iron
distractors: gold is rare in the Earth's crust, not the main metal of the core
aluminium is common in the crust but the core is mostly iron
copper is a useful metal but the core is made mostly of iron
misconceptions: the core is made of gold
the core is made of molten rock not metal
the core is made of aluminium

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: What is the name of the layer of gases held around the Earth by gravity?
answer: the atmosphere
accept: atmosphere
misconceptions: the crust
the mantle
the ozone

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: Describe how the thickness of the crust differs beneath the oceans compared with beneath the continents.
answer: the crust is thinner beneath the oceans (about 5 km)
and thicker beneath the continents (about 30 km)
accept: thinnest under oceans, thickest under continents
around 5 km under oceans and around 30 km under land
misconceptions: the crust is the same thickness everywhere
the crust is thicker under the oceans
the crust is hundreds of kilometres thick

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: A student says 'the whole core of the Earth is molten liquid'. Explain why this is wrong.
answer: only the outer core is molten liquid iron
the inner core is solid because the pressure is so great
accept: the inner core is solid, only the outer part is liquid
the core has two parts: a liquid outer core and a solid inner core
misconceptions: the whole core is liquid iron
the whole core is solid
the core is molten rock all the way through

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: Why do the tectonic plates of the crust float on top of the mantle?
answer: the mantle is denser than the crust
so the less dense plates float on its surface
accept: the crust is less dense than the mantle below it
the plates are lighter (less dense) than the mantle
misconceptions: the crust is denser than the mantle
the mantle is solid so the plates rest on it
the plates float because the mantle is water

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: How does the slowly moving mantle lead to earthquakes?
answer: the slow-moving mantle causes the tectonic plates to move gradually
the movement of these plates at the surface causes earthquakes
accept: moving mantle drags the plates, and moving plates cause earthquakes
misconceptions: earthquakes are caused by the core spinning
earthquakes happen when the crust melts
earthquakes are caused by the atmosphere

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: Which layer of the Earth is made of slow-moving, semi-molten rock?
answer: the mantle
distractors: the crust is the thin solid outer layer we live on, not semi-molten
the inner core is solid iron, not semi-molten rock
the atmosphere is the layer of gases, not rock
misconceptions: the crust is semi-molten
the inner core is semi-molten rock
the atmosphere is part of the solid Earth

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: The Earth is about 12 500 km across, yet humans can only use a tiny fraction of it for resources. Explain why.
answer: only the first few km of the crust (and the atmosphere) can be reached
the vast majority of the Earth is too deep and inaccessible
accept: we can only reach the thin top of the crust, the rest is out of reach
most of the Earth is far too deep to dig into
misconceptions: we can mine all the way to the core
the whole Earth is available for resources
resources only come from the mantle

subtopic: Composition and structure of the Earth
question: The crust is about 5 km thick beneath the oceans and about 30 km thick beneath the continents. How much thicker is the continental crust than the oceanic crust?
answer: 25 km

question: Which gas makes up about 78% of the Earth's atmosphere?
answer: nitrogen
accept: nitrogen (N2)
misconceptions: oxygen
carbon dioxide
argon

question: What percentage of the atmosphere is oxygen?
answer: about 21%
accept: around 21 per cent

question: A student says 'the air is mostly oxygen and carbon dioxide'. Which statement correctly describes the air?
answer: The air is mostly nitrogen, with about 21% oxygen and only 0.04% carbon dioxide.
options: The air is mostly nitrogen, with about 21% oxygen and only 0.04% carbon dioxide.
The air is mostly oxygen, with a little nitrogen and carbon dioxide.
The air is about half oxygen and half carbon dioxide.
The air is mostly carbon dioxide, with some oxygen.
distractors: oxygen is only about 21%; nitrogen at 78% is the main gas
oxygen and carbon dioxide are minor compared with nitrogen
carbon dioxide is only about 0.04%, not a major gas
misconceptions: the air is mostly oxygen
the air is mostly carbon dioxide
oxygen and carbon dioxide make up most of the air

question: What proportion of the Earth's atmosphere is carbon dioxide?
answer: about 0.04%
accept: 400 ppm
about 400 parts per million
misconceptions: about 21%
about 1%
about 4%

question: Which gas makes up about 1% of the atmosphere and is a completely unreactive noble gas?
answer: argon
distractors: nitrogen makes up about 78%, not 1%, and is not a noble gas
oxygen makes up about 21% and is very reactive
carbon dioxide is only about 0.04% and is not a noble gas
misconceptions: nitrogen is the noble gas at 1%
oxygen is a noble gas
carbon dioxide makes up about 1%

question: Why is the Earth's atmosphere described as a mixture rather than a single substance?
answer: it is made of several different gases
the gases are not chemically joined together
accept: it contains more than one gas that are not chemically bonded
different gases mixed together but not reacted
misconceptions: it is a compound of nitrogen and oxygen
it is a single pure gas
the gases are chemically joined into one substance

question: Argon has remained at the same level in the atmosphere since the Earth formed. Explain why.
answer: argon is a noble gas that is completely inert (unreactive)
so it does not get used up or made in chemical reactions
accept: it is unreactive so its amount does not change
being inert, it stays the same because nothing reacts with it
misconceptions: argon is constantly being made by plants
argon is reactive but always replaced
argon is a greenhouse gas that builds up

question: Why is oxygen important for animals, and how is it added to the atmosphere?
answer: oxygen is essential for aerobic respiration in animals
it is made (generated) by photosynthesis in plants and algae
accept: animals need it for respiration; plants make it by photosynthesis
misconceptions: oxygen is made by respiration
plants take in oxygen and give out nitrogen
oxygen is unreactive so animals breathe it out unchanged

question: Name the two gases that together make up about 99% of the Earth's atmosphere.
answer: nitrogen and oxygen
accept: N2 and O2
oxygen and nitrogen
misconceptions: oxygen and carbon dioxide
nitrogen and argon
oxygen and water vapour

question: Although carbon dioxide is only 0.04% of the atmosphere, why is it still considered important?
answer: it plays an important role in regulating the Earth's temperature
it acts as a greenhouse gas / takes part in the greenhouse effect
accept: it helps keep the Earth warm even though there is very little of it
misconceptions: it is needed for animals to breathe
it makes up most of the air
it has no effect because there is so little of it

question: If nitrogen is 78% of the atmosphere and oxygen is 21%, what percentage is left for all the other gases combined?
Why can the exact percentages of gases in the atmosphere change slightly from place to place?
answer: because the atmosphere is a mixture, so there is no fixed ratio (e.g. water vapour varies from 0-4%)
accept: it is a mixture so amounts vary, especially water vapour
water vapour changes with temperature and evaporation
misconceptions: the gases are chemically fixed so cannot change
the percentages never change anywhere
oxygen levels double at night

question: What does it mean to recycle an object?
answer: to use the material an object is made from to make a new object
accept: take the material and use it to make something new
misconceptions: to use the same object over and over for its original purpose
to throw the object away
to repair the object

question: What is the difference between reusing an object and recycling an object?
answer: reusing means using the object again for its intended purpose
recycling means using the material to make a new object
accept: reuse = same object used again; recycle = material made into something new
misconceptions: reusing and recycling are the same thing
reusing means melting it down
recycling means throwing it in a hole

question: What makes a material such as metal or fossil fuel 'finite'?
answer: there is only a limited amount and it cannot be replaced once used up
options: there is only a limited amount and it cannot be replaced once used up
it can be grown again within about 30 years
it is made fresh by plants every year
there is an endless supply in the Earth's crust
distractors: only renewable materials like wood can be grown again in about 30 years
finite materials are not made by plants; renewable ones like potato starch are
the crust is thin and ores are limited, so the supply is not endless
misconceptions: finite materials grow back like trees
finite means there is an unlimited amount
metals are renewable

question: Give three reasons why recycling metals is better than extracting them from fresh ore.
answer: we do not need to dig more holes, which are ugly and destroy habitats
it is cheaper than digging and extracting
it usually uses less energy than extracting metals from ores
accept: less mining/digging
cheaper
uses less energy / better for the environment
misconceptions: recycling makes the metal stronger
recycling creates new metal atoms
recycling is always free

question: Which of these is a renewable material?
answer: wood from trees
distractors: iron is extracted from ore, which is finite and will run out
coal is a fossil fuel and is finite, not renewable
gold is a finite metal and is incredibly rare in the crust
misconceptions: metals are renewable
fossil fuels grow back like trees
all materials can be replaced

question: You plant some trees and chop them down after 30 years, then immediately plant and grow another batch the same way. How many years pass before you can chop down the second batch?
answer: 60 years

question: What name is given to rock that forms when molten rock cools and hardens?
answer: igneous rock
accept: igneous
misconceptions: sedimentary rock
metamorphic rock
molten rock

question: Explain how igneous rocks are formed.
answer: hot molten rock from the mantle
cools and hardens at or just below the surface of the Earth
accept: magma/lava cools and solidifies
misconceptions: formed by heat and pressure on existing rock
formed from compressed sediment
formed when rocks are weathered

question: Granite has large crystals but basalt has very small crystals. Explain why.
answer: granite cooled slowly (often underground) so large crystals formed
basalt cooled quickly (often at the surface) so very small crystals formed
accept: slow cooling gives big crystals; fast/rapid cooling gives small crystals
misconceptions: granite is older so crystals grew bigger
basalt cooled slowly
crystal size depends on the chemicals not the cooling rate

question: What does the word 'morph' mean, as in metamorphic rock?
answer: to change
accept: change
misconceptions: to melt
to break apart
to harden

question: Explain how metamorphic rocks are formed.
answer: existing rocks are changed by heat and pressure under the ground
over millions of years
accept: heat and pressure change existing rock without melting it
misconceptions: formed when molten rock cools
formed from compressed layers of sediment
the chemicals in the rock are changed

question: Describe one feature of the appearance of metamorphic rocks.
answer: a squashed, layered or banded appearance
accept: banded
layered
squashed
misconceptions: large interlocking crystals
soft and crumbly
full of fossils

question: What is the name of the process in which rock is physically broken into small pieces of sediment?
answer: weathering
accept: weathering of rock
misconceptions: transportation
compaction
erosion melting

question: Other than rain, give two things that can weather rock and form sediment.
answer: rivers flowing over rocks
wind
plant roots growing into and breaking the rocks apart
accept: any two of: rivers, wind, plants/roots
misconceptions: heat from the Sun melting the rock
earthquakes shaking the rock
pressure from layers above

question: What is the name of the process by which sediment is carried by water to lakes, seas and oceans?
answer: transportation
accept: transport
misconceptions: weathering
compaction
sedimentation

question: Describe how sediment in the sea is turned into sedimentary rock.
answer: sediment sinks to the bottom
more and more layers are deposited on top
the layers are compressed/cemented under the weight of layers above over millions of years
accept: layers build up and are compacted/squashed over a very long time
misconceptions: the sediment is heated until it melts
heat and pressure change it without compressing
it cools from molten rock

question: State two properties of sedimentary rocks.
answer: often soft and crumbly
porous (water can pass through them)
accept: soft/crumbly
porous/permeable to water
misconceptions: hard and impermeable
made of interlocking crystals
banded or layered by heat and pressure

question: Fossils are usually only found in sedimentary rocks. Explain why.
answer: dead animals get trapped in the sediment as it builds up
sedimentary rock forms by compression without melting, so the imprints are preserved
accept: igneous and metamorphic rocks form from melting/extreme heat which would destroy fossils
misconceptions: fossils are made of igneous rock
heat and pressure preserve fossils best
fossils form when molten rock cools

question: What are the three types of rock?
answer: igneous, sedimentary and metamorphic
accept: igneous; sedimentary; metamorphic
misconceptions: solid, liquid and gas
granite, basalt and marble
molten, weathered and compressed

question: A student says 'once a rock has formed it never changes'. Explain why this is wrong.
answer: the Earth's crust is not static / rocks continually change into one another
over millions of years through processes such as weathering, melting, heat and pressure
accept: all three rock types can change into each other in the rock cycle
misconceptions: only sedimentary rocks can change
rocks only change if a volcano erupts
rocks change in a few years

question: By which process can an igneous rock be turned back into molten rock?
answer: melting
when it is forced/pushed back underground where it is very hot
accept: heating until it melts underground
misconceptions: weathering
compaction
heat and pressure without melting

question: Which process turns igneous and sedimentary rocks into metamorphic rocks?
answer: heat and pressure
distractors: weathering breaks rock into sediment, it does not change rock into metamorphic rock
cooling forms igneous rock from molten rock, not metamorphic rock
compaction squashes sediment to form sedimentary rock, not metamorphic rock
misconceptions: weathering makes metamorphic rock
metamorphic rock forms by cooling molten rock

question: A rock is soft, crumbly and contains a fossil. Which type of rock is it most likely to be?
answer: sedimentary
distractors: igneous rocks are hard, made of interlocking crystals and do not contain fossils
metamorphic rocks are hard and banded and do not contain fossils
'molten' describes liquid rock, not a solid rock type
misconceptions: fossils are found in igneous rock
crumbly rocks are metamorphic

question: Roughly how long do the processes of the rock cycle take?
answer: millions of years
distractors: weathering and rock formation are far too slow to happen in days
rocks do not change type within a few years
even hundreds of years is far too short for rocks to change type
misconceptions: the rock cycle happens quickly
rocks change type within a human lifetime

question: Which gas in the atmosphere contains carbon atoms and moves through the carbon cycle?
answer: carbon dioxide
accept: CO2
misconceptions: oxygen
nitrogen
argon

question: Which biological process removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere?
answer: photosynthesis
distractors: respiration releases carbon dioxide back into the atmosphere, it does not remove it
combustion is burning, which adds carbon dioxide to the atmosphere
deforestation slows removal of carbon dioxide, it is not the process that removes it
misconceptions: respiration removes carbon dioxide
combustion removes carbon dioxide

question: Name two processes that put carbon dioxide back into the atmosphere.
answer: respiration
combustion (burning)
accept: respiration and combustion/burning of fuels
misconceptions: photosynthesis
weathering
transportation

question: Explain how the carbon in a plant ends up inside an animal.
answer: plants make glucose and other biological molecules by photosynthesis
animals eat the plants, turning the sugars into biological molecules in their bodies
accept: the carbon passes along when the animal eats the plant
misconceptions: the animal breathes the carbon in from the air
the carbon comes from drinking water
plants give carbon to animals through their roots

question: Describe how fossil fuels are formed.
answer: plants and animals died millions of years ago and were not eaten
their bodies were buried deep underground
and turned into fossil fuels by extreme pressure and heat
accept: dead organisms crushed underground over millions of years become fossil fuels
misconceptions: fossil fuels form in a few years
fossil fuels are made by photosynthesis
fossil fuels form from cooling molten rock

question: What is deforestation?
answer: cutting down lots of trees
distractors: planting trees is the opposite of deforestation
burning fossil fuels is combustion, a separate human activity
absorbing carbon dioxide is what living trees do by photosynthesis, not deforestation
misconceptions: deforestation means planting trees
deforestation is the same as burning fuels

question: Explain how deforestation increases the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere.
answer: living trees absorb/remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere by photosynthesis
cutting them down slows the rate at which carbon dioxide is removed, so more stays in the atmosphere
accept: fewer trees means less photosynthesis so less CO2 is taken out of the air
misconceptions: cut trees release carbon dioxide by respiring
trees give out carbon dioxide while alive
deforestation has no effect on carbon dioxide

question: Explain how the greenhouse effect keeps the Earth warm.
answer: the Sun's heat energy passes through the atmosphere and warms the Earth's surface
the Earth emits some of this heat energy
greenhouse gases such as carbon dioxide absorb the heat and bounce it back towards the Earth, making it warmer
accept: greenhouse gases trap/re-radiate heat keeping the Earth warmer than it would otherwise be
misconceptions: the gases stop heat from the Sun reaching the Earth
the greenhouse effect heats the Earth directly like a fire
oxygen is the main greenhouse gas

question: A student says 'the greenhouse effect is bad because it makes us too hot'. Explain why they are not fully correct.
answer: the natural greenhouse effect is needed and keeps the Earth warm enough for life
without it the Earth would be too cold to live on; the problem is the extra warming from human-made greenhouse gases
accept: the greenhouse effect itself is natural and necessary; only the extra/enhanced effect is harmful
misconceptions: the greenhouse effect is always harmful
without the greenhouse effect the Earth would be the same
the greenhouse effect makes the Earth too cold

question: Which two human activities have increased the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere?
answer: burning fossil fuels and deforestation
distractors: photosynthesis removes carbon dioxide and respiration is natural, not a human cause of rising levels
planting trees and recycling reduce carbon dioxide rather than increase it
weathering and transportation are rock-cycle processes, not sources of carbon dioxide
misconceptions: respiration is the human cause of rising CO2
planting trees raises carbon dioxide levels

question: Carbon dioxide makes up 0.04% of the atmosphere. In every 100000 air particles, how many would be carbon dioxide?
answer: 40 particles

question: Carbon dioxide is about 0.04% of the atmosphere. The handbook also gives this as parts per million (ppm). How many ppm is 0.04%?
answer: 400 ppm
